evidence: All 10 toolkit tools follow a consistent (session, opts?) -> Promise<ToolResult<T>> signature pattern. Session is always first, options second.
connect() and disconnect() break the ToolResult convention — they use throw/void semantics instead. This is documented and justified (lifecycle vs tool operations).
All tool option types are compact interfaces with 1-4 fields. No function has >5 parameters.
ToolResult<T> envelope is consistently applied: ok/data/error/provenance on every tool return.
ConnectOptions has 14 optional fields — this is the largest API surface, though it is a configuration object rather than a per-call API.
SdkBridge interface has consistent async-only methods with explicit return types for all 12+ methods.
demosError() factory and ok()/err() helpers provide consistent error and result construction.
api_surface_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: ConnectOptions has 14 fields which is a wide configuration surface. The connect/disconnect throw/void pattern vs ToolResult pattern creates a minor two-convention split. The actions/publish-pipeline exports (attestDahr, attestTlsn, publishPost) use raw throws and Demos SDK types instead of the ToolResult pattern.

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The toolkit's public API surface is notably coherent. All tool functions share the same (session, opts) -> Promise<ToolResult<T>> shape, with typed option interfaces and a uniform result envelope. Error construction uses consistent factory helpers. The main irregularity is the connect/disconnect lifecycle boundary, which uses throw/void — but this is explicitly documented and justified. The SdkBridge interface maintains consistent async patterns throughout. The actions/ layer (publish-pipeline) uses a different convention (raw throws, Demos SDK types) but this is an internal boundary rather than a public API.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: A developer using the toolkit can predict API shape from one example — the (session, opts) -> ToolResult<T> pattern holds across all 10 tools. The ToolResult envelope, DemosError codes, and Provenance metadata are used uniformly. The connect/disconnect exception is well-documented. ConnectOptions is wide but serves as configuration, not a per-call API. The score lands at 84.3 — solidly above the 80 threshold for recurring irregularities, with the connect pattern split and wide ConnectOptions preventing a higher score.

issues.summary: actions/publish-pipeline.ts exports use raw throws and SDK types instead of toolkit ToolResult convention
evidence: attestDahr() throws raw Error on failure instead of returning ToolResult with DemosError
publishPost() accepts raw Demos SDK instance and DemosTransactions instead of DemosSession
AttestResult in publish-pipeline.ts has different shape (type, url, requestedUrl, data?: any) than toolkit's AttestResult (responseHash, txHash)
issues.suggestion: Since publish-pipeline.ts is consumed by session-runner (not external consumers), this is lower priority. If toolkit becomes the single API surface, migrate publish-pipeline to use ToolResult pattern and import types from toolkit/types.ts.

root_causes: The actions/ layer predates the toolkit layer. When the toolkit was designed, it established clean conventions but the older actions/ code was not migrated.

add.description: All 10 toolkit tools use the same function signature: session as first param, typed options as second, returning Promise<ToolResult<T>>. This consistency means a developer can predict the API shape of any tool from seeing one.

add.header: ToolResult envelope with typed error codes
add.description: Every tool result carries ok/data/error/provenance. Errors use 10 typed DemosErrorCode values with retryable flag. ok() and err() factory helpers enforce the shape at construction.

The lifecycle boundary (connect/disconnect) deliberately uses different error semantics than tool operations. This is documented and justified — session creation failures are not recoverable via ToolResult.
